learning_outcomes.unit_name: Unit I: Chemical Substances - Nature and Behaviour
learning_outcomes.topic: Classification of Elements and Compound Formation
outcomes: Describes classification of elements in the Periodic Table.
Explains how compounds (including carbon compounds) are formed based on atomic structure (Bohr's model) and properties (valency).

learning_outcomes.unit_name: Unit I: Chemical Substances - Nature and Behaviour
learning_outcomes.topic: Nature and Properties of Chemical Substances
outcomes: Investigates the nature and properties of chemical substances including distillation, crystallisation, chromatography, centrifugation.
Identifies types and properties of mixtures, solutions, colloids, and suspensions.

learning_outcomes.unit_name: Unit I: Chemical Substances - Nature and Behaviour
outcomes: Describes and represents chemical interactions and changes using symbols and chemical equations.
Analyzes acid-base reactions, metal-non-metal interactions, and reversible/irreversible changes.
Applies Newton's laws to explain the effect of forces on state of motion (displacement, direction, velocity, acceleration).
Analyzes graphical and mathematical representations of motion in one dimension including uniform circular motion and acceleration due to gravity.
Explains the relationship between mass and weight using the universal law of gravitation.
Connects gravitational laws to the laws of motion.
Manipulates the position of objects and properties of lenses (focus, centre of curvature) to observe image characteristics.
Corresponds observations with ray diagrams and extends understanding to combinations of lenses (telescope, microscope).
Manipulates and analyses circuit characteristics (current, voltage, resistance) and mathematises their relationship using Ohm's law.
Applies electrical concepts to everyday usage including electricity bills, short circuits, and safety measures.
Defines work in scientific terms and represents relationships between potential and kinetic energy mathematically.
Demonstrates the principle of mechanical advantage by constructing simple machines (levers and pulleys).
Describes the origin and properties of sound (wavelength, frequency, amplitude).
Analyzes differences in sound perception as it propagates through different instruments.
Explores interconnected systems and phenomena supporting life on Earth (hydrosphere, biosphere, atmosphere, geosphere, cryosphere).
Analyzes interrelationships between earth processes and hazards.
Explains the role of cellular components (nucleus, mitochondria, endoplasmic reticulum, vacuoles, chloroplast, cell wall).
Describes the semi-permeability of the cell membrane as the structural basis of living organisms and functional basis of life processes.
Analyses similarities and differences in life processes including nutrition (photosynthesis, absorption, digestion), transport, exchange of materials, and reproduction.
Compares mechanisms across plants, fungi, and animals.
Describes mechanisms of heredity in terms of DNA, genes, and chromosomes.
Explains variation as changes in the sequence of DNA.
Applies knowledge of cellular diversity and ecological roles to classify organisms into five kingdoms.
Illustrates different levels of organisation of living organisms from molecules to organisms.
Analyses different levels of biological organisation from organisms to ecosystems and biomes.
Examines interactions taking place at each level of organisation.
Analyses patterns of inheritance of traits in terms of Mendel's laws.
Evaluates consequences at a population level using models and/or simulations.
Analyses evidence of biological evolution demonstrating consequences of natural selection.
Describes changes in allele frequency, structure, and function of organisms.
